cards.title: English has silent letters
cards.description:
Some English words have letters that are not pronounced, like:
knee
hour
knife

Silent letters are tricky.

cards.title: "e" is the most used letter
cards.description:
The letter E is the most commonly used letter in English words. You can find it in words like:
see
people

cards.title: English words from many languages
cards.description: English has borrowed words from many languages like French, Latin, Korean, and Japanese. That's why some English words can sound and look very different.

cards.title: One letter can have many sounds
cards.description:
English letters can sound different depending on the word.

Example:
a in "name"
a in "car"

cards.title: English is spoken worldwide
cards.description: English is used by millions of people worldwide for communication, school, travel, and entertainment. That's why it is one of the most important international languages today.

cards.title: Zero Was Not Always Used
cards.description: The number zero was not part of many ancient number systems. Today, it is one of the most important numbers in mathematics and technology.

cards.title: English Uses Special Number Words
cards.description: Some English numbers have unique names that do not follow regular patterns, such as eleven and twelve. This is why they must be memorized.

cards.title: Forty Has No "U"
cards.description: Many learners mistakenly write fourty, but the correct spelling is forty. It is one of the most common spelling mistakes in English.

cards.title: Numbers Are Everywhere
cards.description: English numbers are used in daily life for telling time, reading prices, sharing phone numbers, and talking about age.

cards.title: One Hundred Is a Milestone Number
cards.description: The number 100 is often used to represent a complete set, a perfect score, or an important achievement in many countries.

cards.title: English Uses Ordinal Numbers for Dates
cards.description: When reading dates, English uses ordinal numbers such as first, second, and third, instead of regular numbers.

cards.title: Not All Ordinal Numbers End the Same Way
cards.description: Most ordinal numbers end in -th, but some are unique, such as first, second, and third.

cards.title: Month Names Always Start with a Capital Letter
cards.description: In English, month names like January, April, and December always begin with a capital letter.

cards.title: Dates Can Be Written Differently
cards.description: Some countries write dates as Month-Day-Year, while others use Day-Month-Year. The pronunciation usually stays the same.

cards.title: Dates Are Used Every Day
cards.description: People use dates for birthdays, holidays, appointments, school schedules, and many other activities.

cards.title: Days Are Always Capitalized
cards.description: In English, the names of days always begin with a capital letter, such as Monday, Tuesday, and Sunday.

cards.title: Sunday Was Named After the Sun
cards.description: The word Sunday comes from the Sun, while Monday comes from the Moon.

cards.title: There Are 168 Hours in a Week
cards.description: A week has 7 days, and each day has 24 hours, making a total of 168 hours.

cards.title: Weekend Traditions Differ Around the World
cards.description: In many countries, the weekend is Saturday and Sunday, but some countries have different weekend schedules.

cards.title: Days Help Us Organize Time
cards.description: People use days of the week to plan school, work, meetings, holidays, and daily activities.

cards.title: July and August Are Named After Roman Leaders
cards.description: July was named after Julius Caesar, and August was named after Augustus Caesar.

cards.title: February Is the Shortest Month
cards.description: February usually has 28 days, but it has 29 days during a leap year.

cards.title: Month Names Always Start with a Capital Letter
cards.description: In English, month names such as January, May, and December always begin with a capital letter.

cards.title: There Are Four Seasons in a Year
cards.description: Many countries divide the year into four seasons: Spring, Summer, Autumn (Fall), and Winter.

cards.title: Some Holidays Are Named After Months
cards.description: For example, Halloween is celebrated in October, while Christmas is celebrated in December.

cards.title: Hangul Was Created by a King!
cards.description: Hangul was created by King Sejong in 1443 to help ordinary people read and write more easily. Before Hangul, Korean people used difficult Chinese characters.

cards.title: Hangul Is One of the Easiest Alphabets
cards.description: Hangul is known as one of the easiest alphabets to learn because the letters follow simple patterns. Many beginners can start reading Korean in just a few days.

cards.title: K-pop Fans Often Learn Hangul First
cards.description:
Many international fans learning Korean through:
- song lyrics
- idol names
- dramas subtitles

So, Start with Hangul first!

cards.title: Korean Sounds Can Change
cards.description:
Some Korean letters can sound slightly different depending on where they are placed in a word.
Example: ㄱ can sound like "G" or "K".

cards.title: Hangul Has a Special Day in Korea
cards.description: South Korea celebrates Hangul Day every October 9th to honor the Korean alphabet created by King Sejong. Hangul helped many people learn to read and write more easily.

cards.title: Hangul Vowels Follow a Pattern
cards.description: Most Hangul vowels are created using simple lines and dots. Once you understand the pattern, learning new vowels becomes much easier.

cards.title: Korean Has Sounds That Don't Exist in Indonesian
cards.description: Sounds like ㅓ (eo) and ㅡ (eu) can be unfamiliar to Indonesian learners because they do not exist in everyday Indonesian pronunciation.

cards.title: Hangul Was Designed to Be Easy to Learn
cards.description: Hangul was created in 1443 by King Sejong and his scholars so that ordinary people could learn to read and write more easily.

cards.title: Every Korean Syllable Needs a Vowel
cards.description:
Unlike some languages, every Hangul syllable must contain at least one vowel.

Examples:
아 (a)
오 (o)
우 (u)

cards.title: Vowels Help Create Thousands of Korean Words
cards.description: By combining a few vowels with consonants, you can already read and pronounce many basic Korean words.

cards.title: Hangul Consonants Were Inspired by the Mouth
cards.description:
Several Hangul consonants were designed based on the shape of the mouth and tongue when making the sound.

For example, ㄴ represents the tongue touching the upper gums.

cards.title: ㅇ Has Two Different Functions
cards.description:
The consonant ㅇ is silent at the beginning of a syllable, but sounds like "ng" when it appears at the end.

Examples:
아 → a
강 → gang

cards.title: Korean Has Fewer Consonants Than English
cards.description: Modern Korean uses only 14 basic consonants, making them easier to memorize than many learners expect.

cards.title: Consonants Cannot Stand Alone
cards.description:
In Korean, consonants usually need a vowel to create a complete syllable block.

Example:
ㄱ + ㅏ = 가 (ga)
ㄴ + ㅏ = 나 (na)

cards.title: You Can Already Read Simple Korean Words
cards.description: Once you know basic vowels and consonants, you can start reading many simple Korean syllables and words.

cards.title: Compound Vowels Are Made from Basic Vowels
cards.description:
Many Korean vowels are created by combining two basic vowels together.

Example:
ㅗ + ㅏ = ㅘ (wa)
ㅜ + ㅓ = ㅝ (wo)

cards.title: Some Compound Vowels Sound Similar
cards.description: In modern Korean, vowels like ㅐ and ㅔ are often pronounced very similarly by native speakers.

cards.title: Compound Vowels Are Everywhere
cards.description: Many common Korean words contain compound vowels, so learning them will help you read more naturally.

cards.title: Hangul Uses Logical Patterns
cards.description: Once you understand how basic vowels combine, it's easier to remember compound vowels because they follow consistent patterns.

cards.title: Compound Vowels Make Korean More Expressive
cards.description: By combining vowels, Korean can create many different sounds while still using a simple writing system.

cards.title: Double Consonants Sound Stronger
cards.description: Double consonants are pronounced with more tension and emphasis than basic consonants. They are not simply pronounced louder.

cards.title: Korean Learners Often Mix Them Up
cards.description: Many beginners confuse ㄱ and ㄲ, ㄷ and ㄸ, or ㅂ and ㅃ because the differences can be subtle at first.

cards.title: Double Consonants Can Change Meaning
cards.description:
A small pronunciation difference can create a completely different word.

Example:
가 (ga)
까 (kka)

cards.title: Not Every Consonant Has a Double Version
cards.description:
Only five consonants have official double forms:

ㄲ
ㄸ
ㅃ
ㅆ
ㅉ

cards.title: Native Speakers Use Them Every Day
cards.description: Double consonants appear in many common Korean words, making them an important part of everyday communication.
